content.text: The public cloud is a deployment model where cloud computing resources — compute, storage, networking, databases, and services — are owned and operated by a third-party provider and delivered over the internet. Microsoft, Amazon, and Google are the three dominant public cloud providers. Azure is Microsoft's public cloud platform.

"Public" does not mean your data is publicly accessible. It means the underlying infrastructure is shared among many customers (tenants), with logical isolation enforced by the hypervisor, virtual networking, and identity systems. Your VMs, databases, and storage are as private as on-premises — the physical hardware underneath is shared.
Azure is built on a hierarchy of physical and logical groupings. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for designing resilient, compliant workloads.

attrs.alt: Azure Public Cloud global infrastructure: three geographies (Americas, Europe, Asia Pacific), each containing multiple regions with availability zones. Microsoft global network connects all regions. Multi-tenant with logical isolation.
attrs.title: Azure Public Cloud Global Infrastructure

content.text: A geography is a discrete market, typically a country or a group of countries, that contains two or more Azure regions. Geographies preserve data residency and compliance boundaries. Azure has geographies across Americas, Europe, Asia Pacific, Middle East, and Africa.

A region is a set of datacenters deployed within a latency-defined perimeter and connected through a dedicated regional low-latency network. Azure has 60+ regions worldwide — more than any other cloud provider. When you create a resource you choose a region; that choice determines where your data physically resides.
Factors to consider when choosing a region:
Regulations like GDPR require personal data to remain within the EU. Choose North Europe (Ireland) or West Europe (Netherlands) to satisfy this.
Selecting a region close to your end users reduces round-trip latency. For users in Southeast Asia, Southeast Asia (Singapore) or Japan East (Tokyo) is preferable to East US.
Not every Azure service is available in every region. New services often launch in East US and West Europe first, then expand. Check the Azure products by region page before committing.
Prices vary by region due to local infrastructure and energy costs. East US and West Europe are typically the most cost-competitive.

Availability Zones (AZs) are physically separate datacenters within a region. Each zone has independent power, cooling, and network connectivity. If one zone fails (power outage, network fault), the other zones continue operating. Azure guarantees a minimum of three AZs per supported region.
Services that support zone redundancy — Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, App Service, Azure Kubernetes Service, Standard Load Balancer — offer a
when deployed across zones, compared to 99.9% for single-zone deployments.

Azure pairs each region with another region in the same geography, at least 300 km away. If a regional disaster (large-scale outage, natural disaster) affects one region, the paired region is available. Geo-redundant storage (GRS) replicates data asynchronously to the paired region automatically.

The defining characteristic of the public cloud is shared physical infrastructure. Multiple customers' workloads run on the same physical servers simultaneously. Azure enforces isolation through multiple layers:
Azure runs its own custom hypervisor (not Hyper-V directly for cloud VMs). Each customer's VMs are hardware-isolated; a VM cannot read another tenant's memory.
Each customer's VNet is an isolated network overlay. Traffic cannot flow between tenants' VNets without explicit peering. IP ranges can overlap between tenants without conflict.
Each Azure subscription is linked to an Entra ID tenant. Role assignments and permissions in one tenant have zero effect on another.
Storage accounts use cryptographic keys unique to each account. Azure encrypts all data at rest using AES-256 by default; keys are managed per-account or per-customer in Key Vault.
Provisioning Resources in the Public Cloud

In the public cloud, resources are provisioned on-demand via API calls — no procurement, no physical racking. The Azure CLI, Azure Portal, Bicep, Terraform, and the Azure SDK all call the same underlying Azure Resource Manager (ARM) API.

# Choose a region and provision resources in seconds
az group create --name rg-demo --location eastus

# Check which AZs are available in a region
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query "[?contains(name, 'Standard_D2s')].{Name:name, Zones:locationInfo[0].zones}" \
  --output table

# Deploy a zone-redundant App Service
az appservice plan create \
  --name plan-prod \
  --resource-group rg-demo \
  --sku P2v3 \
  --is-linux \
  --zone-redundant  # spans AZ-1, AZ-2, AZ-3 automatically

# Check a service is available in the target region before deployment
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.DocumentDB \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='databaseAccounts'].locations" \
  --output table
Key Benefits of the Public Cloud
You do not buy servers. Resources are provisioned instantly and billed as operating expenditure (pay-as-you-go). This eliminates 3–5 year hardware refresh cycles.
Provision 10,000 VMs in minutes for a batch job; delete them when done. You are never limited by pre-purchased capacity sitting idle.
Deploy to 60+ regions in minutes. A startup can go global on day one without opening datacenters in each country.
Azure offers 200+ services — AI/ML, IoT, analytics, databases, media, security — that would take years to build and maintain in-house.
Datacenters are guarded 24/7, with biometric access, cameras, and physical security audits. You inherit this security without procuring it yourself.
Azure publishes SLAs for every service — 99.9% to 99.999%. Microsoft compensates you with service credits if uptime falls below the SLA.

Some highly regulated industries (defense, certain government agencies) cannot place data in multi-tenant infrastructure regardless of logical isolation. These workloads use Azure Government (a dedicated cloud) or private cloud instead.

Accessing the public cloud requires internet connectivity. For critical operations, organizations use Azure ExpressRoute — a dedicated private connection from their network to Azure, bypassing the public internet.

On burstable VM SKUs (B-series), heavy CPU usage by another tenant on the same host can consume burst credits shared at the physical CPU level. For consistent performance, use non-burstable SKUs (D-series, E-series) or Dedicated Hosts.

Relying on managed services (Cosmos DB, Azure SQL, Service Bus) creates dependency on Azure's pricing, availability, and roadmap. Cloud-agnostic architectures (using open-source databases, Kubernetes) reduce this risk but increase operational complexity.
A major benefit of the public cloud for regulated industries is that Microsoft has already completed the expensive compliance audits. Azure holds certifications including:
HIPAA BAA (healthcare — Business Associate Agreement available)
GDPR compliance assistance tools (Microsoft Compliance Manager, data subject request automation)

These certifications apply to the infrastructure and platform layers. Your application code and data handling remain your responsibility. Running a HIPAA-covered workload on Azure requires a signed BAA with Microsoft and your own compliant application design — Azure being HIPAA-certified does not make your app automatically compliant.

By default, access to Azure services goes over the public internet, protected by TLS. Azure Front Door and Azure CDN cache content at edge points of presence (PoPs) worldwide, reducing latency for end users.

Azure ExpressRoute establishes a private, dedicated connection from your on-premises network to Azure, bypassing the public internet. Circuits are available from 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps. ExpressRoute provides predictable latency, higher security (no internet exposure), and is required for some compliance regimes.

# Create an ExpressRoute circuit
az network express-route create \
  --name er-corp-to-azure \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity \
  --location eastus \
  --bandwidth 1000 \
  --peering-location "Washington DC" \
  --provider "Equinix" \
  --sku-family MeteredData \
  --sku-tier Standard

# After the provider activates the circuit, link it to a VNet gateway
az network vnet-gateway create \
  --name vng-er \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity \
  --location eastus \
  --vnet vnet-hub \
  --gateway-type ExpressRoute \
  --sku ErGw1AZ

az network vpn-connection create \
  --name conn-er \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity \
  --vnet-gateway1 vng-er \
  --express-route-circuit2 er-corp-to-azure

Azure Private Endpoints give services like Blob Storage, Azure SQL, and Cosmos DB a private IP address inside your VNet. Traffic flows over the Microsoft backbone rather than the public internet — even when the service is in the public cloud.

# Create a private endpoint for an Azure SQL server
az network private-endpoint create \
  --name pe-sql \
  --resource-group rg-demo \
  --vnet-name vnet-app \
  --subnet snet-data \
  --private-connection-resource-id \
    $(az sql server show --name mysql --resource-group rg-demo --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id sqlServer \
  --connection-name sql-private-conn

# Disable public network access so ONLY private endpoint works
az sql server update \
  --name mysql \
  --resource-group rg-demo \
  --enable-public-network false
Cost Structure in the Public Cloud
Public cloud billing is consumption-based. Azure offers several pricing models to optimize cost:
Selecting a Region: Practical CLI Workflow

# List all Azure regions and their display names
az account list-locations \
  --query "[].{Region:name, DisplayName:displayName, Paired:metadata.pairedRegion[0].name}" \
  --output table

# Check which VM SKUs are available in a region (and which support AZs)
az vm list-skus \
  --location australiaeast \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query "[?restrictions==[]].{Name:name, Zones:locationInfo[0].zones}" \
  --output table | head -30

# Find the paired region for a given region
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?name=='eastus'].metadata.pairedRegion[0].name" \
  --output tsv
# Output: westus

# Check if a specific service is available in a region
az provider show \
  --namespace Microsoft.CognitiveServices \
  --query "resourceTypes[?resourceType=='accounts'].locations | [0]" \
  --output tsv | grep -i australia

While the standard public cloud serves most customers, Microsoft operates separate dedicated cloud environments for customers with stringent sovereignty requirements. These are separate Azure deployments, not partitions of the same public cloud:

A separate Azure environment for US federal, state, and local government agencies. Operated by screened US persons only. Hosts FedRAMP High, DoD IL2–IL5, and other government-specific certifications. Endpoints end in

Operated by 21Vianet in China to comply with Chinese regulations requiring data to remain in China. Microsoft licenses the technology; 21Vianet operates the infrastructure. Service set is a subset of public cloud.

Within the public cloud, Azure Dedicated Hosts give you an entire physical server (not shared with other customers) for your VMs. This eliminates the noisy-neighbor effect and lets you satisfy compliance requirements that prohibit shared hardware. Pricing is per host per hour regardless of VM utilization.
Azure SLAs are legally binding uptime commitments for each service. If Azure fails to meet the SLA, you receive service credits (a percentage of your monthly bill) — not a refund for damages.
Azure SQL Business Critical, Service Bus Premium
Azure SQL (zone-redundant), Cosmos DB, App Service (zone-redundant)
Cosmos DB (multi-region write), Azure Front Door

Composite SLA: when your workload depends on multiple services, the effective SLA is the product of each component's SLA. An App Service (99.95%) calling Azure SQL (99.99%) has a composite SLA of approximately 99.94% — each additional dependency reduces it. Design for higher availability than the SLA by building retries, circuit breakers, and multi-region failover.
Managing Resources: Subscriptions and Management Groups
Azure organizes resources into a management hierarchy that controls billing, access, and policy:
Container for multiple subscriptions. Apply Azure Policy and RBAC at scale. Large enterprises use management groups to organize subscriptions by environment (prod/dev), business unit, or geography.
The billing boundary. Each subscription has its own invoice and cost quota. Separate production from development/test using separate subscriptions — never only by resource group.
Logical containers within a subscription. Resources in a group share a lifecycle — deploy together, delete together. A resource can belong to only one resource group.
Individual Azure services (VM, storage account, SQL server). A resource exists in exactly one region and one resource group.

# View your subscription hierarchy
az account management-group list --output table

# List all subscriptions in your account
az account list --output table

# Set the active subscription for subsequent CLI commands
az account set --subscription "Production - East US"

# Create a resource group with tags for cost tracking
az group create \
  --name rg-orders-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCenter=Engineering Team=Platform

# List all resource groups in the subscription
az group list --output table
Deploying everything to a single region.
A regional outage (rare but it happens) takes down your entire workload. Use paired regions for disaster recovery and AZs for high availability within a region.
Basic Load Balancer does not support Availability Zones. You must use Standard SKU for zone-redundant deployments. Basic SKU is being retired.
Leaving public endpoints open by default.
Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, and Storage Accounts have public endpoints enabled by default. Always configure service firewall rules or private endpoints to restrict access to your VNet.
Not using Reserved Instances for steady workloads.
If a VM or database runs 24/7 for more than a year, PAYG pricing is 2× the cost of a 1-year Reserved Instance. Review your stable workloads quarterly and reserve them.
Choosing a region for convenience, not compliance.

Deploying EU customer data to East US because your team is on US time violates GDPR. Region selection must account for data residency requirements from day one — migrating data across regions later is complex and expensive.

Think of the public cloud as a city's electrical grid. Many households share the same power plants and distribution infrastructure, but each home has its own meter, circuit breaker, and wiring — your power is isolated from your neighbor's even though you share the generator. You pay for what you use, get a reliable SLA, and never have to maintain the turbines yourself. The trade-off is that you cannot customise the voltage or install your own transformer on the public grid; if you need that level of control, you need a private generator (private cloud).
The public cloud is multi-tenant shared infrastructure with logical isolation — not physically dedicated hardware per customer.
Azure's infrastructure hierarchy: Geography → Region → Availability Zone → Datacenter.
Choose a region based on data residency requirements, user proximity, service availability, and cost.
Use Availability Zones for 99.99% SLA; use Region Pairs for disaster recovery (GRS, geo-failover).
Reduce cost with Reserved Instances (steady workloads) and Spot VMs (fault-tolerant batch).
Private Endpoints and ExpressRoute bring traffic off the public internet while still using the public cloud.
Azure holds 90+ compliance certifications; your application's data handling remains your responsibility.
